abstract: Modern high-performance object detectors increasingly rely on detection proposals to direct their search, effectively bypassing the computational burden of exhaustive sliding-window techniques. While these proposal methods have become a cornerstone of the field, the specific trade-offs they introduce remain poorly understood. In this study, we conduct a comprehensive evaluation of twelve prominent proposal methods and four baselines, examining their repeatability and recall performance across the PASCAL, ImageNet, and MS COCO datasets. We further investigate how these methods influence the end-to-end detection accuracy of frameworks such as DPM, R-CNN, and Fast R-CNN. Our analysis reveals that for effective object detection, enhancing localization precision is just as vital as maximizing raw recall. To capture this relationship, we propose a new metric, Average Recall (AR), which rewards both high coverage and precise spatial alignment. We demonstrate that AR correlates exceptionally well with final detection performance. Our results highlight the systemic strengths and weaknesses of current approaches, providing researchers with the necessary insights and metrics to better select and calibrate proposal methods.
